+---
+assignees:
+- wballard
+position_column: todo
+position_ordinal: fe80
+title: 'End-to-end test: error-severity validator failure blocks the Stop hook (exit 2)'
+---
+**Untested critical path.** When a validator with `severity: error` returns `{"status": "failed", ...}`, avp is supposed to:
+
+1. Mark the rule as a blocking failure in `ExecutedRuleSet::blocking_failures()`.
+2. `validator_executor.rs::handle_ruleset_results` (around line 142) returns `ChainResult::stop(LinkOutput::from_validator_block(...))`.
+3. The chain stops processing.
+4. avp's main exits with code `VALIDATOR_BLOCK_EXIT_CODE` (2).
+5. claude-code interprets exit code 2 as "block this Stop and surface the message to the user."
+
+This whole path has never been exercised end-to-end. Until today every Stop-hook validation passed (because of the missing-content bug). Once `01KQ7EQFSMMYW80HC8PQZ2YATV`, `01KQ35MHFJQPMEKQ08PZKBKFY0`, and `01KQ7KYNBEHQGEMGND4AEG9EV6` land and validators can actually see and read code, the *first time* a real finding lands the block-on-error path will run for the first time. Easy to break and we'd find out the hard way â€” at the worst possible moment (user trying to ship code, gets a confusing hook failure).
+
+## What to test
+
+### 1. Unit: `handle_ruleset_results` produces the right `ChainResult` for an error-severity failure
+
+In `avp-common/src/chain/links/validator_executor.rs`:
+- Construct a synthetic `ExecutedRuleSet` with one `RuleResult { severity: Severity::Error, result: ValidatorResult::fail("magic numbers found") }`.
+- Run `handle_ruleset_results` against it.
+- Assert: returns `ChainResult::stop(...)`, the output contains the rule name + failure message, the exit code is set to `VALIDATOR_BLOCK_EXIT_CODE` for stderr-only hook types.
+
+This unit test covers the chain-link decision logic in isolation.
+
+### 2. Integration: full hook â†’ validator â†’ chain â†’ exit code
+
+In `avp-common/tests/`:
+- Use a `PlaybackAgent` (loaded from a recording fixture, per `01KQ369KBDK6Y5DRN53WB7FDXQ`) where the validator returns `{"status": "failed", "message": "Found magic number 8675309 on line 12"}`.
+- Run the full chain pipeline end-to-end, simulating a Stop hook with a changed file.
+- Capture the chain's final exit code and stdout/stderr output.
+- Assert: exit code is 2, the output includes the rule name and the failure message in a format claude-code can parse.
+
+A fixture for this should be checked in to `avp-common/tests/fixtures/recordings/` â€” a hand-curated `RecordedSession` that returns `{"status": "failed"}` with a specific message. Mirrors the existing `clean_fail` fixture pattern from `01KQ369KBDK6Y5DRN53WB7FDXQ`.
+
+### 3. Manual end-to-end: real qwen run blocks claude-code
+
+After all the prerequisite tasks land, run a real Stop-hook validation against a deliberately-bad Rust file (the `sample_avp_test.rs` fixture from earlier sessions). Confirm:
+- avp exits with code 2.
+- claude-code surfaces the validator's failure message in its UI / stops the response.
+- The user is informed of *which* rule failed and *why*.
+
+This isn't an automated test â€” it's a smoke check before declaring "qwen-as-validator works." Document the result in this card's findings.
+
+## What to verify in the message format
+
+claude-code's hook protocol expects the failure message in a specific shape. The current code in `from_validator_block` produces something â€” verify it matches what claude-code actually parses. Look at:
+- `swissarmyhammer-agent` types for the hook output schema.
+- Any claude-code documentation on hook failure surfaces.
+- Empirically: cause a known-fail in a controlled test and read the rendered output to make sure it's what claude expects.
+
+## Acceptance
+
+- Unit test in `validator_executor.rs` covers the `Severity::Error` â†’ `ChainResult::stop` path with at least three input shapes (single failed rule, multiple failed rules across rulesets, failed rule alongside passing rules).
+- Integration test in `avp-common/tests/` drives the full hook pipeline against a `PlaybackAgent` that fails an error-severity rule, and asserts exit code 2 plus the expected output format.
+- Manual smoke check: real qwen run on a bad Rust file actually blocks claude-code.
+- `cargo test -p avp-common` and `cargo clippy -p avp-common --all-targets -- -D warnings` are clean.
+
+## Pairs with
+
+- `01KQ7EQFSMMYW80HC8PQZ2YATV` (prompts include changed files + diffs). Until validators can see content, they can't fail meaningfully.
+- `01KQ35MHFJQPMEKQ08PZKBKFY0` (always-on tools).
+- `01KQ7KYNBEHQGEMGND4AEG9EV6` (tool name fix).
+- All three need to land first; then this card validates the failure path.
+
+## Why this is a separate card
+
+It's a different layer than the validator-execution work. The validator runs through the runner and produces verdicts. This card validates that downstream of the runner â€” the chain decision, the exit code, the hook output format â€” actually causes claude-code to block. That's chain-link and main-loop territory, distinct from the per-rule session machinery the other tasks touch. #avp
\ No newline at end of file

**Untested critical path.** When a validator with `severity: error` returns `{"status": "failed", ...}`, avp is supposed to:

1. Mark the rule as a blocking failure in `ExecutedRuleSet::blocking_failures()`.
2. `validator_executor.rs::handle_ruleset_results` (around line 142) returns `ChainResult::stop(LinkOutput::from_validator_block(...))`.
3. The chain stops processing.
4. avp's main exits with code `VALIDATOR_BLOCK_EXIT_CODE` (2).
5. claude-code interprets exit code 2 as "block this Stop and surface the message to the user."

This whole path has never been exercised end-to-end. Until today every Stop-hook validation passed (because of the missing-content bug). Once `01KQ7EQFSMMYW80HC8PQZ2YATV`, `01KQ35MHFJQPMEKQ08PZKBKFY0`, and `01KQ7KYNBEHQGEMGND4AEG9EV6` land and validators can actually see and read code, the *first time* a real finding lands the block-on-error path will run for the first time. Easy to break and we'd find out the hard way â€” at the worst possible moment (user trying to ship code, gets a confusing hook failure).

## What to test

### 1. Unit: `handle_ruleset_results` produces the right `ChainResult` for an error-severity failure

In `avp-common/src/chain/links/validator_executor.rs`:
- Construct a synthetic `ExecutedRuleSet` with one `RuleResult { severity: Severity::Error, result: ValidatorResult::fail("magic numbers found") }`.
- Run `handle_ruleset_results` against it.
- Assert: returns `ChainResult::stop(...)`, the output contains the rule name + failure message, the exit code is set to `VALIDATOR_BLOCK_EXIT_CODE` for stderr-only hook types.

This unit test covers the chain-link decision logic in isolation.

### 2. Integration: full hook â†’ validator â†’ chain â†’ exit code

In `avp-common/tests/`:
- Use a `PlaybackAgent` (loaded from a recording fixture, per `01KQ369KBDK6Y5DRN53WB7FDXQ`) where the validator returns `{"status": "failed", "message": "Found magic number 8675309 on line 12"}`.
- Run the full chain pipeline end-to-end, simulating a Stop hook with a changed file.
- Capture the chain's final exit code and stdout/stderr output.
- Assert: exit code is 2, the output includes the rule name and the failure message in a format claude-code can parse.

A fixture for this should be checked in to `avp-common/tests/fixtures/recordings/` â€” a hand-curated `RecordedSession` that returns `{"status": "failed"}` with a specific message. Mirrors the existing `clean_fail` fixture pattern from `01KQ369KBDK6Y5DRN53WB7FDXQ`.

### 3. Manual end-to-end: real qwen run blocks claude-code

After all the prerequisite tasks land, run a real Stop-hook validation against a deliberately-bad Rust file (the `sample_avp_test.rs` fixture from earlier sessions). Confirm:
- avp exits with code 2.
- claude-code surfaces the validator's failure message in its UI / stops the response.
- The user is informed of *which* rule failed and *why*.

This isn't an automated test â€” it's a smoke check before declaring "qwen-as-validator works." Document the result in this card's findings.

## What to verify in the message format

claude-code's hook protocol expects the failure message in a specific shape. The current code in `from_validator_block` produces something â€” verify it matches what claude-code actually parses. Look at:
- `swissarmyhammer-agent` types for the hook output schema.
- Any claude-code documentation on hook failure surfaces.
- Empirically: cause a known-fail in a controlled test and read the rendered output to make sure it's what claude expects.

## Acceptance

- Unit test in `validator_executor.rs` covers the `Severity::Error` â†’ `ChainResult::stop` path with at least three input shapes (single failed rule, multiple failed rules across rulesets, failed rule alongside passing rules).
- Integration test in `avp-common/tests/` drives the full hook pipeline against a `PlaybackAgent` that fails an error-severity rule, and asserts exit code 2 plus the expected output format.
- Manual smoke check: real qwen run on a bad Rust file actually blocks claude-code.
- `cargo test -p avp-common` and `cargo clippy -p avp-common --all-targets -- -D warnings` are clean.

## Pairs with

- `01KQ7EQFSMMYW80HC8PQZ2YATV` (prompts include changed files + diffs). Until validators can see content, they can't fail meaningfully.
- `01KQ35MHFJQPMEKQ08PZKBKFY0` (always-on tools).
- `01KQ7KYNBEHQGEMGND4AEG9EV6` (tool name fix).
- All three need to land first; then this card validates the failure path.

## Why this is a separate card

It's a different layer than the validator-execution work. The validator runs through the runner and produces verdicts. This card validates that downstream of the runner â€” the chain decision, the exit code, the hook output format â€” actually causes claude-code to block. That's chain-link and main-loop territory, distinct from the per-rule session machinery the other tasks touch. #avp
